title: REST endpoint allows TRACE method
description: HTTP TRACE reflects the request body and is exploitable for Cross-Site Tracing (XST).
evidence: HTTP TRACE method is enabled on {{url}} — server returned 200 to a TRACE request.
riskImpact: Cross-Site Tracing (XST) lets an attacker reflect a victim's cookies and Authorization headers — including HttpOnly cookies — back through the browser, bypassing the HttpOnly restriction and enabling session theft.
explanation: HTTP TRACE reflects the request body and is exploitable for Cross-Site Tracing (XST).
fixSteps: Disable TRACE on the server or reverse proxy
In Nginx: return 405 for TRACE requests
In Apache: add TraceEnable Off to httpd.conf
In Express: reject TRACE at the middleware layer before routing

codeExamples.label: Nginx — disable TRACE
Apache — disable TRACE
codeExamples.code:
# In httpd.conf or .htaccess
TraceEnable Off

codeExamples.label: Express.js — reject TRACE

title: REST endpoint allows DELETE
description: REST endpoint exposes DELETE without authentication.
evidence: DELETE method is accepted on {{url}} — server returned a non-405 response to an unauthenticated DELETE request.
riskImpact: Unauthenticated DELETE endpoints allow any internet-reachable client to permanently destroy resources, enabling data-loss attacks or cascading failures in downstream systems that depend on those records.
explanation: REST endpoint exposes DELETE without authentication.
fixSteps: Require authentication on all state-changing verbs (DELETE, PATCH, PUT, POST)
Return 401 for missing tokens and 403 for insufficient scope
Use middleware to enforce auth before any route handler runs

codeExamples.label: Express.js — auth guard middleware
Next.js Route Handler — auth check

title: GraphQL introspection field suggestions
description: Field suggestions on error responses let attackers enumerate the schema.
evidence: GraphQL error response at {{url}} contains a 'Did you mean ...' suggestion, confirming field suggestions are enabled.
riskImpact: Field suggestions in GraphQL error messages let attackers enumerate valid field names one request at a time, reconstructing the schema even when introspection is disabled — exposing internal data models and admin-only fields.
explanation: Field suggestions on error responses let attackers enumerate the schema.
fixSteps: Disable field suggestions using Apollo's formatError hook in production
In Yoga, configure maskedErrors to replace suggestion text with a generic message
Log full errors server-side only; return a generic message to clients

codeExamples.label: Apollo Server 4 — disable suggestions
GraphQL Yoga — masked errors

title: GraphQL schema missing depth-limit directive
description: Without depth-limit, malicious queries can blow up the server with deeply nested queries.
evidence: GraphQL endpoint at {{url}} accepted a deeply nested query (depth > 10) without rejecting it.
riskImpact: Deeply nested GraphQL queries trigger N+1 database round-trips and exponential resolver fan-out, pinning CPU and memory until the server becomes unresponsive — a denial-of-service attack requiring no authentication.
explanation: Without depth-limit, malicious queries can blow up the server with deeply nested queries.
fixSteps: Add graphql-depth-limit and reject queries above depth 5-7
Combine with query-cost analysis to catch wide-but-shallow abusive queries
Return 400 with a clear error when the limit is exceeded

codeExamples.label: Apollo Server — graphql-depth-limit
Express-GraphQL — depth limit

title: GraphQL endpoint has no rate-limit headers
description: GraphQL POST endpoints need rate-limiting because one query can touch many resolvers.
evidence: GraphQL endpoint at {{url}} returned no rate-limit headers (X-RateLimit-*, RateLimit-*) after repeated requests.
riskImpact: A single GraphQL request can fan out to dozens of resolvers, consuming database connections and CPU far beyond what a per-request IP limit catches — letting attackers exhaust server resources with a small number of cheap HTTP calls.
explanation: GraphQL POST endpoints need rate-limiting because one query can touch many resolvers.
fixSteps: Rate-limit by query cost and depth, not just request count
Use graphql-rate-limit or a token-bucket middleware keyed on the authenticated user
Return 429 with Retry-After when limits are exceeded

codeExamples.label: graphql-rate-limit — per-user rate limit
Express — global rate limit for GraphQL endpoint

title: GraphQL persisted queries not enabled
description: Persisted queries (APQ) lock the schema surface to known queries.
evidence: GraphQL endpoint at {{url}} accepted an ad-hoc query string without a persisted-query hash.
riskImpact: Without persisted queries, attackers can send arbitrary GraphQL queries probing every field in the schema. Persisted queries constrain the API to a known allowlist, significantly reducing the attack surface and preventing schema scraping.
explanation: Persisted queries (APQ) lock the schema surface to known queries.
fixSteps: Enable Apollo Automatic Persisted Queries (APQ) on the server
In production, reject non-persisted queries (allowlist-only mode)
Use Relay Compiler to pre-register all client queries at build time

codeExamples.label: Apollo Server — persisted queries (allowlist-only)
Apollo Client — APQ link

title: OpenAPI server URL leaks internal host
description: swagger.json often contains the internal / staging host in the 'servers' array.
evidence: OpenAPI document at {{url}} contains a 'servers' entry with a private or staging hostname.
riskImpact: Internal hostnames in public OpenAPI documents reveal network topology, staging infrastructure, and internal DNS naming conventions — information that aids targeted reconnaissance and phishing attacks against internal services.
explanation: swagger.json often contains the internal / staging host in the 'servers' array.
fixSteps: Generate environment-specific OpenAPI documents; exclude internal hosts from the public document
Use relative server URLs (/ or /api) in the public-facing document
Strip servers[] from public docs using a build-time transform or swagger-combine

codeExamples.label: OpenAPI 3.0 — relative server URL (safe)
codeExamples.code:
openapi: '3.0.3'
info:
  title: My API
  version: '1.0'
servers:
  - url: /api/v1
    description: Current deployment

codeExamples.label: Node.js — strip internal servers at runtime

title: CORS preflight result not cached
description: Missing Access-Control-Max-Age forces browsers to send preflight OPTIONS on every cross-origin request.
evidence: CORS preflight response at {{url}} is missing the Access-Control-Max-Age header.
riskImpact: Without preflight caching, every cross-origin API call from browser clients triggers an extra OPTIONS round-trip, doubling perceived latency for API consumers and increasing server load under heavy traffic.
explanation: Missing Access-Control-Max-Age forces browsers to send preflight OPTIONS on every cross-origin request.
fixSteps: Add Access-Control-Max-Age: 600 (10 minutes) for stable CORS configs
Do not set it above 86400 (24 hours) — a stale policy can block legitimate requests after a config change

codeExamples.label: Express cors() — set max age
Nginx — CORS preflight with max age

title: Bearer token in URL or cookie
description: Authorization: Bearer is fine, but Bearer tokens in URL or cookies can leak via logs.
evidence: Bearer token detected in the URL query string or cookie on {{url}}.
riskImpact: Tokens in URLs appear in server access logs, browser history, Referer headers, and CDN logs — any of which may be accessible to third parties. A single log export or shoulder-surf leaks all active sessions.
explanation: Authorization: Bearer is fine, but Bearer tokens in URL or cookies can leak via logs.
fixSteps: Send Bearer tokens only in the Authorization header
Never accept tokens as query parameters (?token=...) in production endpoints
If cookies are used, set Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Strict and add CSRF protection

codeExamples.label: Correct — Authorization header
Server — reject token in query string

title: CORS preflight required for simple request
description: Non-simple headers (Authorization, X-Custom) trigger a preflight OPTIONS.
evidence: API endpoint at {{url}} requires a CORS preflight due to non-simple headers or methods.
riskImpact: Preflight requests add a full network round-trip before every cross-origin call, increasing perceived latency for browser clients by 50-300 ms depending on geography — a measurable UX impact for latency-sensitive features.
explanation: Non-simple headers (Authorization, X-Custom) trigger a preflight OPTIONS.
fixSteps: Cache preflight results with Access-Control-Max-Age: 600
Consider a session-cookie pattern for browser clients to avoid Authorization headers
Combine multiple cross-origin calls into a single request to minimize preflight overhead

codeExamples.label: Express — maximize preflight caching

title: Rate limit response missing 429 status
description: Rate-limited responses should return HTTP 429 with Retry-After.
evidence: Rate-limited response at {{url}} returned a non-429 status code without a Retry-After header.
riskImpact: Returning 200 or 403 for rate-limited requests prevents clients and API gateways from distinguishing throttling from errors. Attackers can continue probing without knowing they have hit a limit, while legitimate clients cannot back off intelligently.
explanation: Rate-limited responses should return HTTP 429 with Retry-After.
fixSteps: Return HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header when limits are exceeded
Include X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset in all responses

codeExamples.label: express-rate-limit — correct 429 response

title: JSONP callback parameter accepted
description: JSONP allows arbitrary JS to be loaded in the victim's origin. Prefer CORS.
evidence: API endpoint at {{url}} wraps its response in a callback function name supplied via a query parameter.
riskImpact: JSONP endpoints can be embedded as <script src> from any origin, letting attackers steal response data by overriding the callback. They also bypass Content Security Policy and cannot be protected by SameSite cookies.
explanation: JSONP allows arbitrary JS to be loaded in the victim's origin. Prefer CORS.
fixSteps: Disable JSONP support and serve standard JSON with CORS headers instead
If legacy clients require JSONP, validate the callback name against an allowlist of safe identifiers
Never allow arbitrary JavaScript identifiers as the callback value

codeExamples.label: Migrate from JSONP to CORS (Express)

title: CORS origin allow-all
description: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials is unsafe; without credentials it may still be too permissive for an internal API.
evidence: Response from {{url}} contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * — any origin can read the response.
riskImpact: A wildcard origin allows any website to read API responses in the victim's browser. When combined with cookies or credentials, it enables CSRF-style data theft from authenticated sessions across any origin.
explanation: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials is unsafe; without credentials it may still be too permissive for an internal API.
fixSteps: Replace the wildcard with an explicit allowlist of trusted origins
Never combine Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
Dynamically echo the Origin header only after validating it against your allowlist

codeExamples.label: Express — origin allowlist
Nginx — dynamic CORS with allowlist check

title: REST endpoint allows PUT without authentication
description: PUT changes server state. Without authentication, anyone reachable to the endpoint can overwrite resources (config, user profiles, files).
evidence: PUT method succeeded on {{url}} without an Authorization header.
riskImpact: Unauthenticated PUT endpoints allow attackers to overwrite arbitrary resources — user profiles, configuration files, permissions — without any proof of identity. A single request can corrupt data for all users.
explanation: PUT changes server state. Without authentication, anyone reachable to the endpoint can overwrite resources (config, user profiles, files).
fixSteps: Require authentication on PUT and reject anonymous writes at the proxy or framework layer
Validate that the authenticated user owns the resource being updated
Use an allowlist of updatable fields to prevent mass assignment

codeExamples.label: Express — authenticated PUT with ownership check

title: REST endpoint allows PATCH without authentication
description: PATCH partially mutates a resource. Unauthenticated PATCH endpoints allow attackers to flip boolean fields, escalate privileges, or change ownership markers.
evidence: PATCH method succeeded on {{url}} without an Authorization header.
riskImpact: Unauthenticated PATCH endpoints let attackers flip boolean fields (isAdmin, isActive, isVerified), change ownership IDs, or modify billing state — privilege escalation with no brute-force required.
explanation: PATCH partially mutates a resource. Unauthenticated PATCH endpoints allow attackers to flip boolean fields, escalate privileges, or change ownership markers.
fixSteps: Require authentication on PATCH at the middleware layer before any routing
Validate the JSON merge patch against an explicit allowlist of mutable fields
Reject patches to privilege fields (role, isAdmin, isVerified) for non-admin callers

codeExamples.label: Express — safe PATCH with field allowlist

title: OPTIONS response exposes full method allowlist
description: A verbose OPTIONS response (Allow: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) leaks the entire verb surface to attackers, simplifying endpoint enumeration.
evidence: OPTIONS request to {{url}} returned an Allow header listing methods beyond what the endpoint requires.
riskImpact: A verbose Allow header tells attackers exactly which HTTP methods are active, reducing the effort needed to map the API and craft targeted attacks against PUT, PATCH, or DELETE handlers.
explanation: A verbose OPTIONS response (Allow: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) leaks the entire verb surface to attackers, simplifying endpoint enumeration.
fixSteps: Restrict Allow to only the verbs you actually serve on that specific path
Disable unused HTTP methods at the web server or reverse proxy level
Return 405 for any method not explicitly handled

codeExamples.label: Express — explicit method list per route
Nginx — restrict methods

title: GraphQL introspection enabled in production
description: __schema / __type queries dump the entire schema, including internal fields, admin-only mutations, and deprecation hints that aid targeted attacks.
evidence: GraphQL introspection query (__schema { queryType { name } }) returned a full schema dump at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Introspection in production gives attackers a complete blueprint of your data model — every type, field, mutation, and subscription — eliminating the recon phase and exposing internal-only operations that untrusted clients should never call.
explanation: __schema / __type queries dump the entire schema, including internal fields, admin-only mutations, and deprecation hints that aid targeted attacks.
fixSteps: Disable introspection in production (Apollo: introspection: false, GraphQL.NET: ExposeMetadata = false)
Allow introspection only for authenticated admin users if needed for internal tooling
Use persisted queries to give clients what they need without exposing the full schema

codeExamples.label: Apollo Server 4 — disable introspection in production
GraphQL Yoga — NoSchemaIntrospectionCustomRule

title: GraphQL batch (array) queries accepted
description: Accepting an array of queries lets a single HTTP request fan out to N independent operations, multiplying rate-limit cost and bypassing per-request throttles.
evidence: GraphQL endpoint at {{url}} accepted an array of query objects in a single POST body.
riskImpact: A single batched HTTP request containing 100 queries executes 100 independent resolver trees while consuming only 1 unit of any per-request rate limit — a cheap amplification attack that can exhaust database connections and CPU without triggering throttling.
explanation: Accepting an array of queries lets a single HTTP request fan out to N independent operations, multiplying rate-limit cost and bypassing per-request throttles.
fixSteps: Disable batching if not required by clients
If batching is needed, cap the array size (e.g., max 10 items) and charge each item against the rate-limit budget
In Apollo Server: set allowBatchedHttpRequests: false in the HTTP handler options

codeExamples.label: Apollo Server — disable batching
Custom batch size limit middleware

title: GraphQL alias-based depth bypass
description: A naive depth-limit can be evaded with aliases that rename the same field many times. Each alias resolves independently, multiplying cost.
evidence: GraphQL endpoint at {{url}} accepted a query using aliases to repeat the same expensive field multiple times without hitting the depth limit.
riskImpact: Aliases allow an attacker to call the same expensive resolver dozens of times in one query while staying within any depth limit — turning a single request into the equivalent of dozens, triggering N+1 database queries and CPU exhaustion.
explanation: A naive depth-limit can be evaded with aliases that rename the same field many times. Each alias resolves independently, multiplying cost.
fixSteps: Add query-cost analysis alongside depth limits to catch wide-but-shallow alias abuse
Use graphql-query-complexity to assign field costs and reject queries above your budget
Consider capping the maximum number of aliases per query

codeExamples.label: graphql-query-complexity — cost analysis

title: GraphQL error response leaks stack trace
description: Stack traces in error.extensions.stacktrace expose file paths, framework versions, and SQL fragments to any unauthenticated caller.
evidence: GraphQL error response at {{url}} contains a stacktrace field in error.extensions with internal file paths or SQL.
riskImpact: Stack traces embedded in API responses reveal internal file system paths, framework versions, database schema details, and SQL fragments — giving attackers the reconnaissance needed to craft targeted injection or path traversal attacks.
explanation: Stack traces in error.extensions.stacktrace expose file paths, framework versions, and SQL fragments to any unauthenticated caller.
fixSteps: Use Apollo's formatError hook to strip stack traces in production
Log full errors server-side and return only a generic message to clients
Gate stack traces on NODE_ENV — development only

codeExamples.label: Apollo Server — strip stack traces in production

title: GraphQL query cost / complexity not enforced
description: Without cost analysis, a query that joins every table or fires N+1 resolvers can pin a CPU for minutes. Depth-limit alone misses wide queries.
evidence: GraphQL endpoint at {{url}} has no query complexity limits — complex queries are accepted without rejection or throttling.
riskImpact: Without query-cost enforcement, a single unauthenticated GraphQL request that fires N+1 resolvers or joins multiple large tables can consume all available CPU and database connections for several minutes, taking down the service for all users.
explanation: Without cost analysis, a query that joins every table or fires N+1 resolvers can pin a CPU for minutes. Depth-limit alone misses wide queries.
fixSteps: Add graphql-query-complexity or graphql-cost-analysis and reject queries above your budget
Assign per-field and per-list-item cost values that reflect real resolver cost
Combine with depth limits and rate limiting for comprehensive protection

codeExamples.label: graphql-query-complexity — per-field cost estimators

title: OpenAPI security scheme is weak or missing
description: An OpenAPI document with no securitySchemes, or one that lists only apiKey in header, signals that the API does not enforce real authentication.
evidence: OpenAPI document at {{url}} has no securitySchemes defined or declares only an insecure API key scheme.
riskImpact: Missing or weak security schemes mean operations are undocumented from an access-control perspective. SDK generators produce unauthenticated clients, and automated scanners treat every endpoint as public.
explanation: An OpenAPI document with no securitySchemes, or one that lists only apiKey in header, signals that the API does not enforce real authentication.
fixSteps: Declare a strong securityScheme (OAuth2 + JWT or mTLS) in the OpenAPI document
Apply the scheme globally and override per-operation only for truly public endpoints
Use spectral in CI to validate security scheme coverage

codeExamples.label: OpenAPI 3.0 — JWT bearer scheme applied globally
codeExamples.code:
components:
  securitySchemes:
    bearerAuth:
      type: http
      scheme: bearer
      bearerFormat: JWT

# Apply globally — all operations require auth by default
security:
  - bearerAuth: []

paths:
  /users:
    get:
      summary: List users
      # Inherits global bearerAuth requirement
      responses:
        '200':
          description: OK
  /health:
    get:
      summary: Health check
      security: []  # Override: public endpoint
      responses:
        '200':
          description: OK

title: OpenAPI schema declares defaults for sensitive fields
description: Defaults such as isAdmin: false, role: user, or apiKey: REPLACE_ME teach attackers the shape of state and embed credentials in client SDKs.
evidence: OpenAPI schema at {{url}} declares a default value for a sensitive field such as role, isAdmin, or apiKey.
riskImpact: Default values for privilege fields in a public OpenAPI document tell attackers the exact property names and expected values used for access control, enabling targeted mass-assignment attacks to escalate privileges with a single crafted HTTP request.
explanation: Defaults such as isAdmin: false, role: user, or apiKey: REPLACE_ME teach attackers the shape of state and embed credentials in client SDKs.
fixSteps: Remove default values from any field whose name contains token, password, key, role, admin, or secret
Use writeOnly: true to mark privilege fields so SDKs exclude them from generated models
Run spectral in CI to enforce no-defaults on sensitive properties

codeExamples.label: OpenAPI — remove defaults from privilege fields
codeExamples.code:
# Bad: exposes privilege structure
# UserCreate:
#   properties:
#     role:
#       type: string
#       default: user      # remove this
#     isAdmin:
#       type: boolean
#       default: false     # remove this

# Good: no defaults on privilege fields
UserCreate:
  required:
    - email
    - password
  properties:
    email:
      type: string
      format: email
    password:
      type: string
      format: password
      writeOnly: true

title: JWT verifier accepts alg=none
description: A token with alg=none carries no signature. Verifiers that accept it let attackers forge any subject, role, or expiry they want.
evidence: JWT endpoint at {{url}} accepted a token with alg=none in the header without rejection.
riskImpact: An attacker can sign any payload — including admin roles, extended expiry, and arbitrary user IDs — using alg=none. The server accepts it as valid, granting full access to any account or privilege level with no credentials.
explanation: A token with alg=none carries no signature. Verifiers that accept it let attackers forge any subject, role, or expiry they want.
fixSteps: Explicitly pin the accepted algorithm to RS256 or ES256; never accept alg=none
Use a JWT library that rejects the none algorithm by default (e.g., jose in Node.js)
Rotate all existing tokens and signing keys immediately if this has been exploited

codeExamples.label: Node.js jose — pin algorithm, reject none
jsonwebtoken — pinned algorithm

title: JWT HS256 signed with weak or hard-coded secret
description: HS256 trusts a shared secret. If the secret is short, default, or checked into source, attackers can sign forged tokens offline.
evidence: JWT at {{url}} is signed with HS256 and the secret appears to be weak, default, or hard-coded.
riskImpact: A weak HS256 secret (under 256 bits, or a known default like 'secret') can be cracked offline using hashcat in minutes. Once the secret is known, attackers can forge any JWT — impersonating any user, extending expiry, or granting admin access indefinitely.
explanation: HS256 trusts a shared secret. If the secret is short, default, or checked into source, attackers can sign forged tokens offline.
fixSteps: Use at least 256 bits (32 bytes) of random entropy for HS256 secrets
Prefer asymmetric algorithms (RS256, ES256) so the signing key never leaves the server
Rotate secrets and invalidate all existing tokens if a weak or exposed secret is discovered

codeExamples.label: Generate a strong HS256 secret
codeExamples.code:
# Generate a 256-bit (32 byte) random secret
openssl rand -base64 32

# Store as an environment variable — never hard-code it
export JWT_SECRET="$(openssl rand -base64 32)"

codeExamples.label: Switch to RS256 (recommended)

title: JWT issued without exp claim
description: Tokens without an explicit expiry live forever once stolen. Replay attacks succeed indefinitely and revocation is impossible.
evidence: JWT issued by {{url}} does not contain an exp claim in the payload.
riskImpact: A stolen JWT with no expiry is a permanent credential. An attacker who intercepts it — through a log leak, XSS, or MITM — retains access indefinitely with no way to revoke it short of rotating the signing key and invalidating every active session.
explanation: Tokens without an explicit expiry live forever once stolen. Replay attacks succeed indefinitely and revocation is impossible.
fixSteps: Always include an exp claim; keep access tokens short-lived (15 min to 1 hour)
Validate exp server-side on every request
Issue refresh tokens separately with longer lifetimes and implement rotation

codeExamples.label: jsonwebtoken — mandatory expiry

title: CORS allows credentials with wildcard or reflected origin
description: Sending Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true alongside Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * (or a reflected Origin) lets any site issue authenticated requests as the victim.
evidence: Response from {{url}} contains both Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true and Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * (or a reflected origin).
riskImpact: Any attacker-controlled website can initiate cross-origin requests to this API that carry the victim's session cookies, reading authenticated responses. This enables silent data exfiltration, account takeover, and CSRF-equivalent attacks against every logged-in user who visits an attacker page.
explanation: Sending Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true alongside Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * (or a reflected Origin) lets any site issue authenticated requests as the victim.
fixSteps: Replace wildcard with an explicit allowlist and only echo a matching Origin header
Never set Allow-Credentials: true unless the reflected origin is validated against your allowlist
Reject requests where Origin does not match; return no CORS headers to disallowed origins

codeExamples.label: Express — validated origin with credentials

title: CORS reflects Origin: null
description: Echoing the literal string 'null' as Access-Control-Allow-Origin is exploitable because sandboxed iframes, file://, and data: URIs all send Origin: null.
evidence: Response from {{url}} contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin: null when sent a request with Origin: null.
riskImpact: Any attacker can host a sandboxed iframe or data: URI page that sends requests to your API with Origin: null, and the browser will forward the response — including cookies and auth tokens — back to the sandboxed attacker context.
explanation: Echoing the literal string 'null' as Access-Control-Allow-Origin is exploitable because sandboxed iframes, file://, and data: URIs all send Origin: null.
fixSteps: Never echo the literal string 'null' as an allowed origin
Treat null like any other origin — require it to match your explicit allowlist
Return no CORS headers for null origins in non-development environments

codeExamples.label: Express — reject null origin explicitly

title: CORS preflight cache exceeds 24 hours
description: Access-Control-Max-Age above 86400 pins browsers to a stale allowlist. New disallowed headers or origins take up to Max-Age seconds to take effect.
evidence: CORS preflight response at {{url}} has Access-Control-Max-Age set above 86400 seconds (24 hours).
riskImpact: An excessively long preflight cache means CORS policy changes — such as revoking an origin's access or removing a method — take days to propagate to browsers. Previously approved origins continue to receive cached preflight approval even after the server policy changes.
explanation: Access-Control-Max-Age above 86400 pins browsers to a stale allowlist. New disallowed headers or origins take up to Max-Age seconds to take effect.
fixSteps: Cap Max-Age at 600 (10 minutes) for most APIs
Use shorter windows (60s) during active CORS policy rollouts
Most browsers cap Max-Age at 86400 (Chrome) or 600 (Firefox) regardless of what you set

codeExamples.label: Express cors() — safe max age

title: Rate-limit keyed only on client IP, no auth required
description: Per-IP throttling is fine for anonymous traffic, but auth-less endpoints behind a shared NAT or proxy can be exhausted by one noisy neighbour.
evidence: API endpoint at {{url}} enforces rate limits only by IP with no authentication requirement.
riskImpact: IP-based rate limiting can be exhausted by a single user behind a corporate NAT or Tor exit node, blocking all users sharing that IP. Conversely, an attacker rotating IPs or using botnets can bypass it entirely for brute-force or scraping attacks.
explanation: Per-IP throttling is fine for anonymous traffic, but auth-less endpoints behind a shared NAT or proxy can be exhausted by one noisy neighbour.
fixSteps: Require authentication on sensitive endpoints and key rate limits on the authenticated principal (user ID)
Layer rate limits: per-IP for anonymous endpoints, per-user for authenticated endpoints
Use Redis for distributed rate limiting across multiple server instances

codeExamples.label: express-rate-limit — per-user after auth

title: Rate-limit headers present but only enforced on some paths
description: Returning X-RateLimit-* headers without enforcing the cap is theatre. Callers see budgets they cannot exceed in the response but can hammer the server anyway.
evidence: API at {{url}} returns rate-limit headers but continued accepting requests after the stated limit was reached.
riskImpact: Advertising rate limits that are not enforced creates a false sense of security. Automated scanners and attackers will ignore the headers and keep sending requests, while the server has no actual protection against abuse or DoS.
explanation: Returning X-RateLimit-* headers without enforcing the cap is theatre. Callers see budgets they cannot exceed in the response but can hammer the server anyway.
fixSteps: Ensure every endpoint that emits X-RateLimit-* headers actually enforces the limit
Test rate limiting in CI by sending requests above the stated limit and verifying 429 responses
Apply rate limit middleware globally before route handlers rather than per-route to avoid gaps

codeExamples.label: Express — global rate limit applied before all routes

title: SOAPAction header injection / SSRF
description: If the SOAPAction header is passed verbatim to a downstream HTTP call without validation, attackers can pivot the request to internal services.
evidence: SOAP endpoint at {{url}} forwards the SOAPAction header value to a downstream URL without validation.
riskImpact: An unvalidated SOAPAction header passed to outbound HTTP calls can redirect requests to internal services, cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254), or other hosts the server can reach but the attacker cannot — a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) attack.
explanation: If the SOAPAction header is passed verbatim to a downstream HTTP call without validation, attackers can pivot the request to internal services.
fixSteps: Strict-allowlist the SOAPAction value against known operation names
Never concatenate SOAPAction or any user-supplied string into outbound URLs
Use a pre-built SOAP client library that validates operations against the WSDL

codeExamples.label: Node.js — allowlist SOAPAction values

title: SOAP/XML parser has external entities enabled (XXE)
description: XML parsers with DOCTYPE / external entity processing enabled allow attackers to read local files, hit SSRF endpoints, and exfiltrate via DTD.
evidence: SOAP endpoint at {{url}} processed a DOCTYPE or external entity reference in the request body.
riskImpact: XML External Entity (XXE) attacks can read arbitrary files from the server filesystem (/etc/passwd, .env files, application configs), trigger SSRF against internal networks, and in some parsers enable remote code execution via Java serialization gadgets.
explanation: XML parsers with DOCTYPE / external entity processing enabled allow attackers to read local files, hit SSRF endpoints, and exfiltrate via DTD.
fixSteps: Disable DOCTYPE declarations and external entity processing in every XML parser
Use a hardened DocumentBuilderFactory / SAXParserFactory with all external features off
In Node.js, use fast-xml-parser with processEntities: false

codeExamples.label: Java — hardened JAXP parser (no XXE)
Node.js fast-xml-parser — disable entity processing

title: WSDL publicly accessible
description: Auto-published WSDL files enumerate every operation, parameter type, and binding - a blueprint for crafting SOAP-level attacks.
evidence: WSDL document is publicly accessible at {{url}}?wsdl without authentication.
riskImpact: A publicly accessible WSDL gives attackers a complete catalog of every SOAP operation, parameter name, type, and service binding — reducing the effort to discover injection points, craft malformed requests, and understand internal service architecture.
explanation: Auto-published WSDL files enumerate every operation, parameter type, and binding - a blueprint for crafting SOAP-level attacks.
fixSteps: Restrict ?wsdl behind authentication or IP allowlisting
Remove auto-publishing of WSDL from production endpoints; distribute via authenticated developer portals
Consider whether external clients need WSDL at all, or if a static copy can be distributed out-of-band

codeExamples.label: Nginx — restrict WSDL access to internal network
Apache CXF — disable WSDL auto-publishing
codeExamples.code:
<jaxws:endpoint id="myService"
    implementor="com.example.MyServiceImpl"
    address="/MyService">
  <jaxws:properties>
    <entry key="publish-wsdl-information" value="false"/>
    <entry key="org.apache.cxf.service.list.enabled" value="false"/>
  </jaxws:properties>
</jaxws:endpoint>

title: WebSocket upgrade does not validate Origin
description: A WebSocket handshake that ignores the Origin header lets any malicious site open an authenticated WS connection and stream events to the victim.
evidence: WebSocket upgrade at {{url}} accepted a connection with an Origin header from an unrecognized domain.
riskImpact: Without Origin validation, any malicious website a victim visits can silently open an authenticated WebSocket connection using the victim's session cookies, then subscribe to their real-time events — a CSRF equivalent that is invisible to the user.
explanation: A WebSocket handshake that ignores the Origin header lets any malicious site open an authenticated WS connection and stream events to the victim.
fixSteps: Validate the Origin header against an allowlist during the HTTP upgrade handshake
Reject the connection with HTTP 403 if Origin is not in the allowlist
Additionally require a CSRF token in the initial WS message for critical endpoints

codeExamples.label: ws library — Origin validation on upgrade
Socket.IO — built-in CORS origin check

title: WebSocket has no max message size
description: Without a max message size, a single client can pin server memory by sending an oversized frame, or trigger decompressor-bombs if payloads are gzipped.
evidence: WebSocket server at {{url}} accepted a message exceeding a reasonable size limit without rejection.
riskImpact: A single WebSocket client can send a multi-gigabyte frame that pins all available memory on the server until processed or OOM-killed. With per-message compression enabled, a small compressed payload can decompress to many times its size (decompressor bomb).
explanation: Without a max message size, a single client can pin server memory by sending an oversized frame, or trigger decompressor-bombs if payloads are gzipped.
fixSteps: Set maxPayload to a sane limit (64 KB for chat, 1 MB for file chunks) in the WebSocket library
Close the connection with code 1009 (message too big) when the limit is exceeded
If using per-message compression, pair it with a decompressed payload size limit

codeExamples.label: ws library — maxPayload option

title: WebSocket has no idle / disconnect timeout
description: Idle WS connections accumulate as file descriptors and sockets. Eventually the process hits FD limits and refuses new connections.
evidence: WebSocket server at {{url}} kept a connection open indefinitely with no pings or idle timeout.
riskImpact: Without idle timeouts, clients that open WebSocket connections and stop sending data will exhaust the server's file descriptor limit, causing new connections to be refused for all users — a low-effort denial-of-service attack.
explanation: Idle WS connections accumulate as file descriptors and sockets. Eventually the process hits FD limits and refuses new connections.
fixSteps: Send ping frames every 30 seconds and close the connection after two missed pongs
Set an explicit idle timeout (60-120 seconds) to close unresponsive connections
Monitor open connection counts and alert when they approach the FD limit

codeExamples.label: ws library — ping/pong heartbeat

title: POST endpoint accepts no Idempotency-Key
description: Without an Idempotency-Key header, network retries can double-charge payments, duplicate records, or fire emails twice.
evidence: POST endpoint at {{url}} does not accept or honor an Idempotency-Key header.
riskImpact: Network timeouts followed by client retries can create duplicate records, double-charge payment cards, or send duplicate emails and notifications — data corruption that often cannot be safely reversed and damages user trust.
explanation: Without an Idempotency-Key header, network retries can double-charge payments, duplicate records, or fire emails twice.
fixSteps: Accept an Idempotency-Key header on POST /payments and other mutation endpoints
Store the key-result pair and return the cached response on duplicates for at least 24 hours
Use UUID v4 as the key format and validate it on arrival

codeExamples.label: Express — idempotency middleware with Redis

title: REST endpoint mass-assignment risk
description: Endpoints that bind a request body directly to a model (e.g., user.role, isAdmin, internalId) without an explicit allowlist let clients escalate privileges.
evidence: API endpoint at {{url}} appears to accept and bind arbitrary request body fields without filtering.
riskImpact: Mass assignment lets an attacker send unexpected fields (role: 'admin', isVerified: true, creditBalance: 99999) in a JSON body and have them persisted directly to the database record — privilege escalation requiring only a crafted HTTP request.
explanation: Endpoints that bind a request body directly to a model (e.g., user.role, isAdmin, internalId) without an explicit allowlist let clients escalate privileges.
fixSteps: Use explicit DTOs or field allowlists; never spread req.body directly onto ORM models
Mark internal fields (role, isAdmin, isVerified) as non-writable in the schema
Validate and strip unknown properties at the boundary using Zod, Joi, or similar

codeExamples.label: Zod — strict DTO with unknown field rejection

title: REST list endpoint missing pagination headers
description: List endpoints without X-Total-Count or Link: rel="next" force clients to fetch until empty, enabling unbounded dumps and denial-of-service via huge queries.
evidence: List endpoint at {{url}} returned a large payload with no pagination headers or page size constraints.
riskImpact: Without enforced page size caps, a single GET /users or GET /records request can return millions of rows, consuming all available memory and triggering a denial of service — or silently dumping the entire dataset to any authenticated caller.
explanation: List endpoints without X-Total-Count or Link: rel="next" force clients to fetch until empty, enabling unbounded dumps and denial-of-service via huge queries.
fixSteps: Enforce a maximum page size (e.g., 100) and default page size (e.g., 20)
Return Link: rel=next/prev and X-Total-Count headers so clients can paginate safely
Reject requests with limit above the cap with 400 Bad Request

codeExamples.label: Express — paginated list with safe limits

title: REST endpoint missing ETag / conditional GET
description: Without ETags, clients re-download unchanged resources, wasting bandwidth and amplifying scrape traffic.
evidence: GET response from {{url}} does not include an ETag or Last-Modified header.
riskImpact: Without ETags, every client request triggers a full response from the server even when the resource has not changed, wasting bandwidth, increasing server load, and making it easier for scrapers to repeatedly dump data without any conditional-request overhead.
explanation: Without ETags, clients re-download unchanged resources, wasting bandwidth and amplifying scrape traffic.
fixSteps: Emit a strong ETag on GET responses (hash of the response body or last-modified timestamp)
Handle If-None-Match and return 304 when the ETag matches
In Express, use the etag package; in Nginx, ETags are emitted by default

codeExamples.label: Express — ETag with conditional 304 response

title: GraphQL subscription missing per-field auth
description: Subscriptions bypass query-time authorization checks. A user subscribed to onUserUpdated can be fed events for other tenants if authZ is on the resolver, not the source stream.
evidence: GraphQL subscription endpoint at {{url}} does not appear to validate authorization on the subscribe step or per emitted event.
riskImpact: A subscriber can receive real-time events intended for other users or tenants if authorization is checked only at the query resolver rather than the async iterator — enabling cross-tenant data leakage in every event pushed over an authenticated subscription.
explanation: Subscriptions bypass query-time authorization checks. A user subscribed to onUserUpdated can be fed events for other tenants if authZ is on the resolver, not the source stream.
fixSteps: Validate authentication and authorization in the subscribe function, not just the resolver
Re-check authorization on every event emitted by the async iterator before pushing to the client
Filter events server-side so only events owned by the authenticated user are yielded

codeExamples.label: Apollo Server — auth on subscribe and per-event

title: REST response missing _links / HATEOAS navigation
description: Without discoverable links in responses, clients hard-code URLs and break when paths change. Not a security issue but a maintainability one.
evidence: REST response from {{url}} contains no _links or Link header for navigation to related resources.
riskImpact: Without hypermedia links, every API client must hard-code URLs. When paths change (versioning, restructure, CDN migration), all clients break simultaneously and must be updated and redeployed — a coupling problem that slows down API evolution.
explanation: Without discoverable links in responses, clients hard-code URLs and break when paths change. Not a security issue but a maintainability one.
fixSteps: Embed HAL _links or JSON-LD @id fields so clients follow relations rather than constructing URLs
At minimum, return a Link: rel=self header on GET responses
Consider the HAL specification or JSON:API for structured hypermedia

codeExamples.label: HAL-style _links in Express

title: API endpoint has no rate limiting
description: The API endpoint does not enforce rate limiting, allowing unlimited requests from a single client.
evidence: No rate-limit headers (X-RateLimit-*, RateLimit-*) detected in responses from {{url}} after repeated requests.
riskImpact: Without rate limiting, attackers can send unlimited requests to brute-force authentication, scrape all data, enumerate resources, or trigger denial of service — all without any server-side throttling.
explanation: Rate limiting protects APIs from abuse by capping the number of requests a client can make in a time window. Without it, automated attacks against authentication, search, or data endpoints are unconstrained.
fixSteps: Apply a rate-limit middleware to all API endpoints
Key limits on the authenticated user ID for authed endpoints, or IP for anonymous ones
Return 429 with Retry-After and RateLimit-* headers when the limit is hit

codeExamples.label: express-rate-limit — basic setup
Nginx — rate limiting with burst

title: OPTIONS method exposes verbose method list
description: The OPTIONS method is available and returns a verbose Allow header listing all enabled HTTP methods.
evidence: OPTIONS request to {{url}} returned Allow header: {{evidence}}
riskImpact: A verbose Allow header tells attackers exactly which HTTP verbs are active on the endpoint, reducing the reconnaissance effort needed to identify PUT, PATCH, or DELETE handlers for targeted attacks.
explanation: An HTTP OPTIONS request returns the Allow header listing all methods the server accepts on that path. This is useful for CORS preflight but also useful for attackers mapping the API surface.
fixSteps: Restrict the Allow header to only methods that endpoint actually serves
Disable OPTIONS on endpoints that do not need CORS
Return 405 for any method not in the Allow list

codeExamples.label: Express — explicit Allow header per route
Nginx — restrict HTTP methods

title: SOAP endpoint detected
description: A SOAP/XML web service endpoint was detected. SOAP services have a unique attack surface including XXE, SSRF via SOAPAction, and schema enumeration via WSDL.
evidence: SOAP envelope or Content-Type: text/xml response detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: SOAP endpoints are susceptible to XML External Entity (XXE) attacks, SOAPAction injection leading to SSRF, and schema enumeration via publicly accessible WSDL files — attacks that can exfiltrate files, pivot to internal networks, or reveal internal service architecture.
explanation: SOAP services use XML for messages and are susceptible to a class of vulnerabilities distinct from REST APIs: XXE, SOAPAction injection, WSDL enumeration, and WS-Security misconfigurations.
fixSteps: Disable DOCTYPE and external entity processing in the XML parser
Restrict ?wsdl access to authenticated or internal clients only
Validate SOAPAction against a strict allowlist of known operations
Enable WS-Security for authentication and message integrity

codeExamples.label: Java — XXE-safe SAX parser factory

title: XML-RPC endpoint exposed
description: An XML-RPC endpoint is publicly accessible. In WordPress and similar CMSes, xmlrpc.php enables brute-force amplification attacks and remote method invocation.
evidence: XML-RPC endpoint detected at {{url}} — server returned a valid XML-RPC response.
riskImpact: WordPress xmlrpc.php allows an attacker to test thousands of username/password combinations in a single HTTP request using system.multicall — a brute-force amplification attack. It also enables pingback-based SSRF and DDoS amplification against third-party hosts.
explanation: XML-RPC is a legacy remote procedure call protocol used by WordPress for the mobile app and Jetpack. When left publicly accessible it enables brute-force amplification (one request = thousands of auth attempts), pingback SSRF, and remote content modification.
fixSteps: In WordPress: disable xmlrpc.php unless specifically required by a plugin or mobile app
Block xmlrpc.php at the web server or WAF level for non-WordPress applications
Use a security plugin (Wordfence, iThemes Security) to block XML-RPC at the application level

codeExamples.label: Nginx — block xmlrpc.php
Apache — block xmlrpc.php
codeExamples.code:
<Files xmlrpc.php>
    Order Deny,Allow
    Deny from all
</Files>

codeExamples.label: WordPress — disable XML-RPC via filter

title: HTTP TRACE method enabled
description: HTTP TRACE method is enabled on the server. TRACE reflects the full request including headers, enabling Cross-Site Tracing (XST) attacks.
evidence: HTTP TRACE request to {{url}} returned 200 with the request reflected in the response body.
riskImpact: Cross-Site Tracing (XST) exploits TRACE to reflect the victim's cookies and Authorization headers — including HttpOnly cookies that JavaScript cannot read — back through the browser in a cross-origin context, enabling session theft even when HttpOnly is correctly set.
explanation: HTTP TRACE is intended for diagnostic loop-back testing but is rarely needed in production. When enabled, it can be exploited via XST to steal credentials from authenticated users.
fixSteps: Disable TRACE at the web server or reverse proxy level
In Apache: add TraceEnable Off to httpd.conf
In Nginx: return 405 for TRACE requests
In Express: reject TRACE in a global middleware before routing

codeExamples.label: Nginx — disable TRACE
Apache — TraceEnable Off
codeExamples.code:
# In httpd.conf
TraceEnable Off

codeExamples.label: Express.js — reject TRACE globally
